zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House於1500年1月出生在英國英格蘭貝德福爾亨洛福時，他的父親Edward Marshe30歲，母親Lady Isabel Saville26歲。他於1505年與Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe在英國英格蘭貝德福爾亨洛福結婚。他們至少是1個女兒的父母。他去世於1573年在英國英格蘭越可爾達頓享年73歲。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House于1500年1月出生在英国英格兰貝德福爾亨洛福时，他的父亲Edward Marshe30岁，母亲Lady Isabel Saville26岁。他于1505年与Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe在英国英格兰貝德福爾亨洛福结婚。他们至少是1个女儿的父母。他去世于1573年在英国英格兰越可爾達頓享年73岁。

nameMeanings.name: Marshe
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: variant of   March  .

variants.name: March
meanings: English: habitational name from March (Cambridgeshire) or from any of numerous minor English placenames deriving from Middle English marche ‘boundary’ (partly from Old English mearc in the dative-case form mearce, partly from Old French marche). Compare Mark 2.
Catalan: from the personal name March, Catalan equivalent of Mark 1. This surname is also found in southern France.
German: topographic name from Middle Low German march, Middle High German marc(h) ‘mark, sign, boundary’, or a habitational name from a place called with this word (Bavaria).

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: possibly a metonymic occupational name for a fisherman, from orfe, a species of freshwater fish. Alternatively, a habitational name from Urff near Kassel, formerly Orpha.

variants.name: Orff
meanings: German: variant of   Orf  .

nameMeanings.name: Dey
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

variants.name: De
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Day
meanings: English: occupational name from Middle English day(e), dey(e) ‘dairyman or dairymaid’. Originally used only of women, it was later used of men with the sense ‘man in charge of the dairy cattle’. This is probably the most common source of the surname.

English: from the Middle English personal name Day(e) or Dey. In western England this is probably a pet form of David , but in northern England and perhaps elsewhere also it is a late Middle English variant of Daw, a pet form of Ralph (see Daw , Dakin ).
Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Deaghaidh (see O'Dea ).

nameMeanings.name: or
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Jewish (Israeli): from Hebrew or ‘light’, a Hebraicized form of various original Ashkenazic surnames.
Chinese: Cantonese form of the surname 柯, see   Ke   1.
Chinese: Hokkien form of the surname 胡, see   Hu   1.

variants.name: Ke
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Heng, Yong, Bin, Song, Yan, Zhi, Cheng, Chih-Ming, Chun-Yen, Fan, Fong, Gang.  Vietnamese Lan, Hai, Hoa, Le Van, Mui, Tuy.

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 柯, meaning ‘bough, stalk’ in Chinese: (i) from the first element of the personal name Ke Lu (柯盧), personal name of a prince of the state of Wu during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ). (ii) from the first element of the placename Ke Ze (柯澤), the name of a fief (located in Shandong province) granted to an official in the state of Qi during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ). (iii) traced back to the Qiang ethnic group in ancient western China and the Xianbei ethnic group in northern China. (iv) adopted as a surname by the Ke Ba (柯拔) family from the Xianbei ethnic group in northern China during the Northern Wei dynasty (386–534  AD ).
Chinese: variant of   Ge  .

nameMeanings.name: Dairy
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Irish: variant of   Daly  . In Ireland and Britain this form of the surname is found mainly in Scotland.

variants.name: Daly
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Kieran, Dermot, Brigid, Aileen, Declan, Finbarr, Fintan, Aidan, Brennan, Bridie, Cathal.
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Dálaigh ‘descendant of Dálach’, a personal name based on dál (modern dáil) ‘meeting, assembly’.
English (of Norman origin): variant of   Dally   2.

nameMeanings.name: House
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: topographic or occupational name for someone employed ‘at the house’, from Middle English hous(e) (Old English hūs), probably a religious house such as a convent. In the Middle Ages the majority of the population lived in cottages or huts rather than houses.
English: variant of   Howes  .

English: perhaps a topographic name from an unrecorded Middle English huse, a southwest dialect form of Old English hyse ‘place overgrown with (water) plants’, a derivative of Old English hos ‘(water) plant’. In modern English, the name might have been absorbed into Huss or Hose .

variants.name: Huizenga
meanings: West Frisian and Dutch (mainly Groningen): habitational name from Huizinge in Groningen, a placename possibly composed with Middle Dutch huus, huse, Dutch huis ‘house’ (see House ).

variants.name: Houze
meanings: Altered form of   House   4, a surname of German origin (see   Haus  ).
French (Houzé): metonymic occupational name for a maker or seller of boots and shoes or a nickname for someone noted for his footwear, from Old French ho(u)se ‘boot, shoe’.

variants.name: Howes
meanings: English:

pronounced to rhyme with hose, a variant of Howe 1 with plural or post-medieval excrescent -s. Hose (Leicestershire), recorded as Howes in 1086, is named with the plural of Old English hōh ‘hill spur’.
pronounced to rhyme with cows, a variant of Howe 2 (see Hugh ), with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: Howse
meanings: English (Oxfordshire):
variant of   House  .
variants of   Howes  .

variants.name: Houseman
meanings: English: occupational name from Middle English hous ‘house’ + man ‘man’, denoting someone employed at ‘the house’, probably a religious house or convent. Compare House .
English: altered form of Oulsnam (itself a variant of Wolstenholme ), with metathesis of final -nam to -man and development of inorganic initial H-.
Americanized form of German   Hausmann   or Dutch   Huisman  .

variants.name: Huse
meanings: North German: from Middle Low German hūs ‘house’ (see House 1), which may also refer to an important building such as the town hall, a castle, or a manor house, so that the name would indicate dwelling in close proximity to these, or perhaps it is an epithet for a servant who worked at a great house.
English (Berkshire): variant of   Hose   and possibly a shortened form of   Hussey  .

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From Old Norse, originally a byname meaning ‘bride, wife’; in mythology the name of Thor's golden-haired wife.

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House was born in January 1500, in Henlow, Bedfordshire, England, United Kingdom, his father, Edward Marshe, was 30 and his mother, Lady Isabel Saville, was 26. He married Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe in 1505, in Henlow, Bedfordshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 daughter. He died in 1573, in Darton, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 73.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House est né en janvier 1500, à Henlow, Bedfordshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, son père, Edward Marshe, avait 30 ans et sa mère, Lady Isabel Saville, avait 26 ans. Il a épousé Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe en 1505, à Henlow, Bedfordshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fille. Il est décédé en 1573, à Darton, Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 73 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House im Januar 1500 in Henlow, Bedfordshire, England, United Kingdom geboren wurde, war sein Vater Edward Marshe 30 und seine Mutter Lady Isabel Saville 26. Er hat 1505 in Henlow, Bedfordshire, England, United Kingdom Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Tochter. Er starb 1573 im Alter von 73 Jahren in Darton, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House è nato il nel gennaio 1500 nel Henlow, Bedfordshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, il suo padre, Edward Marshe, aveva 30 anni e il suo madre, Lady Isabel Saville, aveva 26 anni. Lui sposato Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe nel 1505, nel Henlow, Bedfordshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlia. Morì nel 1573, nel Darton, Yorkshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 73 anni.

ja.lifeSummary: Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy Houseが1500年1月にイギリスイギリスベドフォードシュアイアヘンロウで生まれた時，父Edward Marsheは30歳，母Lady Isabel Savilleは26歳であった。1505年にイギリスイギリスベドフォードシュアイアヘンロウでLady Margaret Agnes Walker Marsheと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも1人の娘の親であった。彼は1573年にイギリスイギリスヨークシュアイアダルトンで73歳で死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House(이)가 1500년 1월 영국 잉글랜드 베드퍼드실르 헨로에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Edward Marshe(은)는 30세였고, 그의 어머니 Lady Isabel Saville(은)는 26세였다. 그는 1505년 영국 잉글랜드 베드퍼드실르 헨로에서 Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1573년 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르 더르턴에서 73세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House nasceu em janeiro de 1500, em Henlow, Bedfordshire, Inglaterra, United Kingdom, seu pai, Edward Marshe, tinha 30 anos e sua mãe, Lady Isabel Saville, tinha 26 anos. Ele casou-se com Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe em 1505, em Henlow, Bedfordshire, Inglaterra, United Kingdom. Eles tiveram pelo menos 1 filha. Ele faleceu em 1573, em Darton, Yorkshire, Inglaterra, United Kingdom, com 73 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Sir Thomas Richard Robert Marshe of Dey or Dairy House родился январь 1500 г. в местности хенлоу, бедфордшир, Англия, Великобритания, его отцу по имени Edward Marshe было 30 лет, а матери, которую звали Lady Isabel Saville, 26. Он женился на женщине по имени Lady Margaret Agnes Walker Marshe в 1505 г. в местности хенлоу, бедфордшир, Англия, Великобритания. У них была по крайней мере 1 дочь. Он умер в 1573 г. в местности дартон, еркшир, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 73 лет.
